speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape seven with Morris Rubenstein. Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and a lady from our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when it was taken? Or how old are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1945, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think it might have been earlier. Were you a boy-- how old were you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe 18 or 19. [LAUGHS] I forgot already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a-- a-- another Jewish person took it from us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me. This in Germany, I took.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe 20, 21 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a Jewish person took it, too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hershel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was taken-- my aunt had it, you know, in United States. And I sent her a letter if she has a picture of my father. And she send it to me. That's the only thing I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture which the Jewish people work. It's from a ghetto, and they're loading the-- the truck with dirt. This I found in a German house in-- in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Polands took out some cities from the Germans, you know? I found this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the ghetto that you were in, in Rozyszcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like it. But it's not Rozyszcze. It's like Lithuania, I think. You know, or the-- how the Jews-- how he's dressed, the Jewish person, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who was in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me and a friend on a farm, you know, when we were on the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country are you in here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And around what year is this in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think '46 or '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the guys from the-- from the kibbutz took it, the Jewish-- a Jewish person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me in a market. I worked here in Detroit. I think this was in 19-- 50-- 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, the boat I came to Boston, to United States, from Germany, the

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think '49. It was the end of '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- OK, and who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And around what year is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I think, '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this my family, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- [LAUGHS] I think it's five or six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whose wedding is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna's and Barry's.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- here in Detroit. But it's in-- in Bloomfield, I guess, in the Shul LA, whatever you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture, starting on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is [? Sheila ?] and her baby. And this is Toba and Bernie, and me. And that's our grandson, Eli, and Hannah, [? Barry, ?] Goldi, my wife, David, Nissih, Yosi, and [? Shifra ?] [? Razel ?] and her baby. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken? Who's wedding is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: David's and [? Shoshana's. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me who's in this picture, starting on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is Sheila, the baby, and her husband, and Hannah and [? Barry. ?] And there's the little kids, our grandchildren. And this is Goldi, my wife, and [? Shoshana ?] and David, and Nissih, and Toba, our daughter, Eli, and [? Shifra ?] [? Razel. ?] And this is Bernie and the baby and his wife, [? Lisa. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This our older son, Hershel, and his wife, [? Ruha. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It might be about 11 years ago, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, in the wedding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Rubenstein, will you please introduce your family? You can start with the row in the back.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. David and [? Shoshana ?] his wife. Hannah and [? Barry ?] her husband. Bernie and [? Lisa, ?] his wife. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. [? Mikey ?] is their son, Bernie and-- Bernie and [? Lisa's ?] son. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everyone talk. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Eli, is our grandson, Toba's son. This is my wife, Toba, our daughter, Nissih, our grandson. Goldi, our granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And in the back row, which are by-- which are your children by birth? Who is your son and daughter? Can you just tell me that?

speaker: interviewee
text: David and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hannah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannah, Bernie and Toba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I don't know if any of you would like to say anything, or Miriam first, if you would like to say anything about Mr. Rubenstein doing this interview, what it means to you. You don't have to say anything, but you're more than welcome to say something if you would like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to say that, being a survivor and married to a wonderful survivor for 42 years-- we just celebrated our anniversary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And these are our children and grandchildren that's living in this area. Our others in California and New York. And I'm very thankful for everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And if any of the children would like to say anything about your dad, you're more than welcome to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not a speaker. I just want to say that my parents, they did a wonderful job to raise us. And they always gave us what we needed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anyone else like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this is-- is a interesting situation because, as we grew up, my dad never really mentioned much of what transpired during his childhood, his upbringing. And this will be interesting for us, too. As we've gotten older, my dad's brought out more and more of what's happened. So it'll be interesting for us to see, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, my wife and I just went to Washington, DC. And we were at the Holocaust Memorial. And I guess it-- it makes me feel proud that, you know, my dad went through everything and made it, especially when you see so many people that didn't. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? SHOSHANA ?] RUBENSTEIN: I just want to thank him very much for having the courage to talk today and to use this as a way to teach for generation after generation, not just for the family here, but for really, for all of mankind, and to share his experiences, how difficult, the most difficult experiences that a human being can suffer and willing to share that with everyone and-- and have that as a tool for-- for the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anyone else like to speak? And Morris? Is there anything else you'd like to say, Mr. Rubenstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say? I said, already, enough. But I have to thank God for everything, for my children, grandchildren. And they're all my life. [SOBBING] They're what I live for. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I'm sorry. Are we done?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nope. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just, getting back to-- I think everybody should go to the Holocaust Memorial, because there was a lot of things that kind of hits home, especially being a-- a son of a survivor. But to see so many people that didn't have the will-- and not knocking them, because we weren't there to see what really happened-- but didn't have the will to live. And then seeing your father, having the will just to live and to survive, really makes him a special individual. And speaking for everybody here in the family, I know everybody feels that they're proud to have him as a father. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say we are very lucky to have our-- my mother too, very proud that she's part of our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you gonna say something, Hannah?

speaker: interviewee
text: You've said it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
